<title>SALSA: A Simulation Tool to Assess Ecological Sustainability of Agricultural Production </title>

In order to assess the ecological sustainability of agricul- tural production systems, there is a need for effective tools. We describe an environmental systems analysis tool called SALSA (Systems Ana/ysis for Sustainable Agriculture). It consists of substance/material flow models in which the simulation results are interpreted with life- cycle assessment methodology. The application of SAL- SA is demonstrated in a case study in which three different ways of producing pigs are compared with respect to energy input and the environmental impacts of global warming, eutrophication, and acidification. The scenario that combined a low-protein diet without soy meal with an improved manure-management technique with low nitrogen losses was the best for all impact categories studied. The strength of the SALSA models was their capacity to capture consequences of manage- ment options that had an influence on several processes on a farm, which enabled the type of complex studies we describe.
1: Since the Second World War, agriculture in Sweden has undergone substantial changes. Because of mechanization, the number of people working within agriculture has decreased dramatically and farms have become much more specialized. In addition, the intensity of production has increased. In arable farming, the use of mineral fertilizers has led to higher yields, and likewise at animal farms production levels are now much higher than before. However, there are drawbacks because in many cases this development has led to increased environmental impacts and to a greater dependence on nonrenewable resources such as fossil fuels and phosphates.
At a national scale, today's agriculture contributes signifi- cantly to important environmental impacts such as global warming, eutrophication, and acidification (1, 2). In addition,

the use of nonrenewable resources is relatively high. In order to identify possibilities for improvement, it is therefore important to be able to assess how different ways of producing food affect the environmental impacts. These ways include, for example, different crops, technologies, and management practices.

We describe a simulation tool that has been developed for environmental systems analysis of the ecological sustainability of agricultural production systems. The tool is called SALSA (Systems Analysis for Sustainable Agriculture) and can simulate both arable and animal production. In this approach, ecological sustainability is dealt with in terms of resource consumption and environmental impacts. To demonstrate the usefulness of the tool, we describe a case study in which different alternatives for the production of pigs are compared. Pig meat is the most common type of meat consumed in Sweden. Approximately 35 kg of pig meat is consumed per capita annually, and most of it is produced in Sweden (3). Within the pig meat production chain (from primary production, via the food industry and market phases, to and including the consumer), primary agricultural production constitutes the largest part of many environmental impacts (4).

In pig production, feed composition has a large impact on the environmental performance, because of its consequences for the environmental impacts from both the feed production and the subsequent feed consumption (5, 6). By choosing feed with a low environmental impact during production, and by formulating feeds with a well-balanced amino acid content (without excess protein) that allow the animals to grow well, some of the environmental burdens of meat production can be reduced.

Management practices also influence the ecological sustain- ability of crop and animal production. Manure management is a key factor in this respect, and by using storage and spreading techniques that minimize ammonia losses, high nitrogen (N) utilization efficiency can be achieved. This reduces the need for fertilizer inputs, while also lowering hazardous emissions. Nitrogen fertilizer rates should ideally be adjusted to match

5: The basic approach to looking at production on a farm as a system is illustrated in Figure 1. The farm system requires inputs such as energy, fertilizer, and feed to enable it to produce food in particular, and sometimes also other raw materials or energy crops. The production causes impacts on the external environment in terms of emissions of substances to both water and air. Depending on how the production is organized and managed, resource consumption, level of production, and environmental impacts can be determined, which in turn reflect the ecological sustainability properties.

One way of assessing the sustainability would be to take measurements of indicators and collect other data at a farm, as a basis for calculations. However, this would be impractical, expensive, or sometimes even impossible. Furthermore, this would not allow analyses of hypothetical systems. Instead, a more feasible alternative is to construct a simulation model in which the material and substance flows, and their interactions with the surrounding environment, are represented. The simulations then generate the data needed for the sustainability analysis. In this way, different alternatives for production can easily be handled by changing parameters or submodels in the model.

The methodological approach of the environmental systems analysis tool SALSA is to utilize material/substance flow models complemented by life-cycle assessment (LCA) method- ology (10). The substance flow models are named SALSA- arable (11), SALSA-soybean (5), SALSA-pig (5), and SALSA- cow (12), and they were constructed with MATLAB/Simulink software (13). MATLAB/Simulink is a platform with a graph- ical interface and hierarchical structure and is very well suited for the type of models required.
The LCA methodology is used to set the scope of the models

consumption and environmental impacts. These results are then related to the level of production SO that, for example, the energy consumption is given per kilogram of product. In LCA terminology, this amount of product is called the "functional unit." In the present case study, the functional unit used was 1 kg average pig growth in the interval 29-115 kg.

An important feature of the approach used is that the environmental impacts of producing input materials are in- cluded in the analysis. This means, for instance, that the effects of producing soy meal for animal feed in Brazil and trans- porting it to Sweden are included. Otherwise, environmental impacts could be "exported" from the farm by choosing imported feed, which would make comparisons with feed produced at the pig farm itself unfair. Another example concerns N. If N losses at the farm were reduced, environmental impact from production of artificial fertilizer would become lower because the demand would be lower.

The consumption of input materials considered in SALSA involves energy use (fossil fuel and electricity), artificial fertilizer, and, when applicable, animal feed. The emission substances calculated are nitrate (NO3), nitrous oxide (N20), ammonia (NH3), nitrogen oxides (NOx), sulfur oxides (SOx), phosphorus (P), carbon dioxide (CO2), and methane (CH4). These emissions generally contribute to more than one environmental effect, and, according to the methods used in LCA, they are weighted and aggregated to calculate the following categories: global warming potential, acidification potential, and eutrophication potential, which are some of the most relevant impact categories for agriculture (14).

10: Data on production of Brazilian soybean and the subsequent extraction and distribution of soy meal were taken from an LCA study of pig meat production (16). These data were used in combination with data on soil emissions and transport emissions to construct the SALSA-soybean model.
11: The SALSA-pig model was used to calculate the feed intake, and the resulting impact from feed production, for 1 kg growing-finishing pig. The model also quantifies energy use and emissions from pig-farming processes. The processes included in SALSA-pig were operation of buildings; emissions from manure excretion, manure storage, and manure spreading; tractor emissions from manure spreading; and avoided emis- sions from fertilizer production corresponding to the amount of crop-available N in the manure. Data on production of synthetic amino acids were taken from an LCA report on methionine in poultry production (17).
12: The case-study system deals with the production of slaughter pigs. The pigs were studied at the growing-finishing stage, from 29 kg to the final weight of 115 kg. The study included processes involved in pig husbandry and local production of feed ingredients, where the manure from the pigs was used. Production of agricultural supplies in the form of mineral fertilizer, electricity, diesel, and imported feeds (soybean meal, rapeseed meal, and synthetic amino acids) was also included. The processes studied are shown in Figure 3.

Three alternative scenarios of pig production were designed and simulated using the SALSA-arable, SALSA-soybean, and SALSA-pig models: i) conventional feed (SOY), ii) conven- tional feed with improved management (SOY + and iii) domestic feed with improved management (PEA Since manure handling is known to be a critical factor in pig
production, this was used as the key factor defining the improved management case.

The conventional feed was assumed to be partly based on soy meal imported from Brazil. It consisted of barley (43%), wheat (40%), rapeseed meal (5%), soy meal (8%), synthetic amino acids (0.13%), and premix (3.5%) with a crude protein level of 14.7%. The domestic feed, based on locally produced crops, consisted of barley (41%), wheat (40%), rapeseed meal (7%), peas (8.5%), synthetic amino acids (0.25%), and premix (3.5%) with a crude protein level of 13.7%.

Improved management was defined as 100% N fertilizer rate to crops compared to crop uptake (for wheat, barley, rapeseed) instead of 150%, which is an assumption of the current practice on animal farms. Furthermore, the improved management case employed a band-spreading technique with slurry incorporation within 4 hr for slurry spreading, instead of a broad-spreading technique with slurry incorporation after 24 hr.

14: The simulation results for the three scenarios are given as primary energy requirement, global warming potential, eutro- phication potential, and acidification potential in total and from different parts of the systems. These results are illustrated in Figure 4. For energy use, cereal production was the most important source. The energy saved in the cases SOY and PEA mainly derived from savings on mineral fertilizer production. When soybeans were excluded from the diet (PEA+), some additional energy savings could be made. Likewise, for global-warming potential, cereal production was the most prominent source. The improvement potential was again related to avoided mineral fertilizer production, but also to decreased nitrous oxide emissions from soils as a result of lower fertilizer rates. In the case of eutrophication, cereal production was an important source, in addition to manure emissions in the SOY case. Eutrophication from crop pro- duction was, at normal fertilizer rates, to a large extent determined by the background N leaching from agricultural soils (SOY + and However, at higher fertilizer levels (SOY), N leaching derived from applied N rapidly increased. The difference in manure emissions between the cases SOY and SOY + was related to better management, i.e., lower losses in SOY the additional improvement achieved with PEA + was an effect of lower protein levels in the feed, leading to a lower N content in the manure. Finally, from the results for acidification it is evident that manure emissions were the dominant impact. 16: The simulation results for the three scenarios are given as primary energy requirement, global warming potential, eutro- phication potential, and acidification potential in total and from different parts of the systems. These results are illustrated in Figure 4. For energy use, cereal production was the most important source. The energy saved in the cases SOY and PEA mainly derived from savings on mineral fertilizer production. When soybeans were excluded from the diet (PEA+), some additional energy savings could be made. Likewise, for global-warming potential, cereal production was the most prominent source. The improvement potential was again related to avoided mineral fertilizer production, but also to decreased nitrous oxide emissions from soils as a result of lower fertilizer rates. In the case of eutrophication, cereal production was an important source, in addition to manure emissions in the SOY case. Eutrophication from crop pro- duction was, at normal fertilizer rates, to a large extent determined by the background N leaching from agricultural soils (SOY + and However, at higher fertilizer levels (SOY), N leaching derived from applied N rapidly increased. The difference in manure emissions between the cases SOY and SOY + was related to better management, i.e., lower losses in SOY the additional improvement achieved with PEA + was an effect of lower protein levels in the feed, leading to a lower N content in the manure. Finally, from the results for acidification it is evident that manure emissions were the dominant impact. 18: The overall conclusion was that the scenario combining domestic feed with improved management (PEA was the most favorable among all aspects studied and that the conventional feed in combination with improved management (SOY+) was the second best. The ranking of manure management or feed choice as the most important factor depended on the level of manure mismanagement versus the difference in protein level and proportion of imported feedstuffs in the feeds compared. In the setup assumed here, manure management was shown to be the most important factor.

It can also be seen that the energy used directly at the pig farm was comparatively low. Instead, it was the feed pro- duction, especially the cereal production, that required most of the energy. Similar results have been found by Cederberg and Darelius (16), that feed production was the largest source of energy use in pig meat production at farm level.

The acidification is largely determined by the manure emissions (ammonia). The results showed the potential to reduce the ammonia emissions by using better manure- management techniques, which in this study led to substantially lower acidification and eutrophication. Since lower ammonia emissions also result in lower losses of N, more N can be supplied to crops, which in turn leads to decreased global

warming because the production of mineral fertilizer was avoided. These two findings were confirmed in a British study in which the band-spreading technique was found to be environmentally preferable to broad spreading, and that this advantage was maximized if the farmer made full use of the available N in the manure (18). Another conclusion is that in a system with a relatively high crude protein level of the feed, the effects of poor manure management are amplified, i.e. a large N turnover leads to an elevated risk of nitrogen emissions. The importance of reducing nitrogen emissions from animal production has been stressed by Cederberg (4), for example, who states that losses of reactive N compounds are important not only for eutrophication, acidification, and climate change, but also for the use of energy (via the energy- intensive production of mineral N fertilizers).

21: The systems analysis approach, as exemplified by this study based on the SALSA models, enabled changes in the two complex interacting subsystems (feed production and pig management) to be viewed concurrently. The SALSA models thus proved to be useful tools for environmental evaluation of different feeding strategies, as well as of manure-management practices for pig fattening in a total system perspective. The SALSA models have also previously been used in a study examining the impact of feed choice (19). In the present application, the combined effect of changing both feeding strategy and manure management was investigated, which demonstrates some of the flexibility of the models.

However, it should be noted that this kind of environmental analysis based on material/substance flow models only covers issues related to flows of resources and emissions. There are certainly other environmental consequences that might be important, such as degradation of land and biodiversity. For example, it is known that the production of soybeans in Brazil

has severe consequences in this regard (20), SO these aspects should also be considered in the evaluation of scenarios using soy meal. Moreover, models are always limited by their input data, parameter settings, and model assumptions, etc., and the uncertainties related to these form a field where improvements need to be made. Some of these aspects are discussed by Huijbregts (21), who has studied the uncertainty and variability in environmental life-cycle assessments.

Depending on the purpose of the study, different methods to assess ecological sustainability can be used. As an example, a recently published doctoral thesis describes a study on the level of dependence of Swedish animal production on foreign ecosystems (22). The method used was Appropriated Ecosystem Area, which is based on an extensive collection of data on feed consumption, raw-material use, and production volumes per hectare. Another example is a study by Engström et al. (23), in which the environmental impacts of the Swedish agricultural sector were assessed. The authors mainly used the method Environmentally Extended Input-Output Analysis, which is based on trade between producers and users in different sectors. The latter example can only give a coarse description of the physical flows in agriculture, but can instead cover a larger share of the activities in the sector.

Analogously, the method used in the work with the SALSA models has also been applied in other areas. The most similar example is the waste-management model ORWARE, which also utilizes a MATLAB/Simulink platform in which physical flows are modeled and finally evaluated with LCA methodology (24).

Different approaches contribute to the understanding of the circumstances for ecological sustainability in the food-pro- duction system. The strength of the SALSA models lies in their ability to model consequences of different management options for a selection of physical flows and to present these in the form of quantified environmental impacts. In this way, the environ- mental performance of new management strategies can be tested before farmers implement the strategies at real farms.
